lessons.title: Introduction to physical computing — the Raspberry Pi Pico
lessons.objectives:
I can describe what physical computing is
I can explain what a microcontroller and an embedded system are
I can tell an input device from an output device
I can build and test a working circuit with an LED
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: physical computing · microcontroller · embedded system · GPIO pin · input · output · circuit. TA: every pair has 1 Raspberry Pi Pico on a breadboard, a micro USB cable, Thonny open and showing MicroPython, an LED, a 50 ohm resistor, jumper cables and a button. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — what is a microcontroller? Think, write, pair, share. On the starter worksheet pupils CHOOSE what a microcontroller is and SORT everyday devices into “has an embedded system” / “not really”.
   • Support: tick “a microcontroller is…”.  • Core: name one device at home with a microcontroller.  • Challenge: explain why a washing machine is an embedded system.
2. (5–20) I-do → we-do — meet the Pico and its GPIO pins. Show the board (embedded image); name the USB port, the BOOTSEL button, the microcontroller chip and the GPIO pins. LABEL the board on the starter worksheet (drag the words on, no writing). Input sends a signal IN to the Pico (button); output sends a signal OUT (LED).
   • Likely error: “the Pico IS the computer” → fix-words “the Pico is the microcontroller — a tiny computer on one chip.”
3. (20–45) You-do — build a circuit. On the activity worksheet pupils build the LED circuit (LED long leg → resistor → GP15; short leg → GND), put the blink program in order (Parsons), then run it. Stress: current flows ONE way through an LED; everything needs a path back to GND.
   • Support: choose what value(1) / value(0) does.  • Core: complete the blink loop.  • Challenge: add a button so the LED only lights when pressed. Paste a screenshot of the working circuit. TA: “prompt, do not wire it for them.”
4. (45–55) Plenary — quick-fire questions (input or output? what is GPIO? what is an embedded system?). Self-tick the 4 “I can…”. TA notes who got a circuit working.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Working with motors — the motor controller board
lessons.objectives:
I can explain why motors need a separate motor controller board
I can name the parts of the motor wiring (controller, motors, battery pack, common rails)
I can write code to drive a motor forwards and backwards
I can work out which motor is left and which is right
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: motor controller · L298N · IN1–IN4 · OUT1–OUT4 · common rail · ground (GND) · battery pack. TA: each pair has their Pico, an L298N motor controller, two DC motors, a battery pack (4×AA), jumper cables, a screwdriver, Thonny open. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — the power of GPIO. Recall: there are 26 GPIO pins but fewer GND pins — how do we share? Pupils CHOOSE the answer (a common rail on the breadboard) on the starter worksheet.
   • Support: tick “a motor needs…”.  • Core: why can’t the Pico power a motor directly.  • Challenge: explain what a common ground rail is for.
2. (5–25) I-do → we-do — the motor controller. Motors need MORE power than the Pico can give, so a separate board (and battery pack) drives them; the Pico only sends the data signals (IN1–IN4). Show the wiring image. Pupils ORDER the wiring steps on the activity worksheet (terminals → motors → IN pins to GP12–15 → common rails → battery). Build it.
   • Likely error: motors wired to the wrong IN pins → fix-words “IN1/IN2 go to GP12/GP13, IN3/IN4 to GP14/GP15.”
3. (25–50) You-do — test and configure. Put the motor-test program in order (Parsons), run it, and find out which motor is left/right and which pin is forwards/backwards. Then SORT pin signals into “drives forwards” / “drives backwards”.
   • Support: choose what value(1) does to a motor.  • Core: complete the test code for the second motor.  • Challenge: make both motors spin forwards together. Paste a screenshot. TA: “prompt, do not screw it in for them.”
4. (50–55) Plenary — how would you make the buggy spin on the spot, or zigzag? Self-tick the 4 “I can…”. Homework: collect chassis materials (recycling) for next lesson. TA notes who got both motors driving.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Chassis design and build
lessons.objectives:
I can plan where each component will sit on my buggy
I can choose materials that are strong enough for the job
I can build a chassis that holds together when the buggy moves
I can test my buggy with a simple forwards and backwards program
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: chassis · prototype · structural integrity · aesthetics · power bank. TA: each pair has their Pico project from last lesson, the recycled materials they collected, scissors/tape, Thonny open. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud. This is a hands-on making lesson — minimal code.
1. (0–10) Starter — preparing to build. What does a good chassis have to do? Pupils SORT statements into “a strong chassis” / “a weak chassis” and plan/sketch where the motors, board, battery and sensors will go on the activity worksheet.
   • Support: tick what the chassis must hold.  • Core: name one material and why.  • Challenge: explain why a smooth ‘third wheel’ is needed at one end.
2. (10–40) You-do — build the chassis. ORDER the build steps first (plan → mark out → cut/fix the base → attach motors → add the third wheel → mount the board and battery). Then build it. The chassis must hold the weight AND stay together when it moves.
   • Support: a parts checklist to tick off.  • Core: build to the plan.  • Challenge: make it tidy and decorate it. TA: “prompt for a smaller, sturdier base first.”
3. (40–50) Test it. Run last lesson’s forwards/backwards code on the buggy. Does it hold together and move straight? If it wobbles, fix and test again. Untethered? You need a separate power bank. Paste a screenshot/photo of the buggy.
4. (50–55) Plenary — reflect: did it work as planned, and what would you change? Self-tick the 4 “I can…”. TA notes which buggies are stable enough to carry on.
Design/make lesson — differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Going ultrasonic! — adding a distance sensor
lessons.objectives:
I can explain how ultrasonic sound waves are used to sense distance
I can name the four pins of an ultrasonic sensor and what they do
I can wire an ultrasonic sensor to the Raspberry Pi Pico
I can run code that measures the distance to an object
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: ultrasonic · sound wave · sensor · Trig · Echo · VCC · GND · distance. TA: each pair has their buggy from last lesson, an HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor, four female-to-male jumper cables, the pinout handout, Thonny open. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — what do a bat, a dolphin and a submarine have in common? They all use ultrasonic sound to ‘see’ their surroundings (echolocation). Pupils CHOOSE the answer and SORT things into “uses ultrasonic to sense” / “does not” on the starter worksheet.
   • Support: tick what ultrasonic means.  • Core: explain echolocation in one line.  • Challenge: why a robot uses it instead of a camera.
2. (5–20) I-do → we-do — add the sensor. Show the HC-SR04 (embedded image). MATCH each of the four pins to its job: VCC=power, GND=ground, Trig=send the ping, Echo=hear it bounce back. Wire it: Trig→GP20, Echo→GP21, VCC→red rail, GND→blue rail. Pupils ORDER the wiring steps on the activity worksheet, then build.
   • Likely error: Trig and Echo swapped → fix-words “Trig sends, Echo hears — GP20 then GP21.”
3. (20–50) You-do — test it. Put the ultrasonic test program in order (Parsons), run it, and read the distance as objects move closer/further. The sensor times how long the ping takes to come back; distance = time × speed of sound ÷ 2.
   • Support: choose what the Trig pin does.  • Core: complete the print(distance) line.  • Challenge: make the buggy stop when something is within 10 cm. Paste a screenshot. TA: “prompt, check the pins first.”
4. (50–55) Plenary — how could you combine the motors AND the sensor to avoid bumping into things? Self-tick the 4 “I can…”. TA notes who got a distance reading.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Follow me! — reflective optical sensors and line following
lessons.objectives:
I can explain how a reflective optical sensor works
I can wire two optical sensors to the Raspberry Pi Pico
I can read sensor values to tell if the buggy is on or off the line
I can use the sensor values to steer the buggy along a line
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: reflective optical sensor · infrared · reflect · potentiometer · digital signal (0/1) · autonomous. TA: each pair has their buggy, two reflective optical sensors, six female-to-male jumper cables, a printed black line/track, Thonny open. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — wiring recap + how does it work? An optical sensor shines infrared light DOWN and measures how much bounces back: black line = little reflection, white floor = lots. Pupils CHOOSE what the sensor reads and MATCH the three pins (G, V+, S) to their jobs on the starter worksheet.
   • Support: tick what the sensor shines.  • Core: black line gives a 0 or a 1?  • Challenge: explain what the potentiometer adjusts.
2. (5–20) I-do → we-do — add two sensors. Show the sensor (embedded image). Wire each: GND→blue rail, VCC→red rail, OUT→GP10 (left) and GP11 (right). Run the test code and read “Left: 0/1  Right: 0/1” while covering each sensor. Pupils put the test code in order (Parsons).
   • Likely error: both OUT wires to the same pin → fix-words “left sensor GP10, right sensor GP11.”
3. (20–50) You-do — line-following challenge. Use the steering rules: both off the line → drive forwards; right sensor ON the line → turn right; left sensor ON the line → turn left. Pupils SORT each sensor situation to the right motor action, then write/refine the code and test on the track.
   • Support: match each situation to the action.  • Core: complete the ‘drive forwards’ branch.  • Challenge: make it follow a curve. Paste a screenshot. TA: “prompt, adjust the potentiometer if it is too twitchy.”
4. (50–55) Plenary — quick GPIO quiz (Pin.OUT vs Pin.IN; value(1) vs value(0)). Self-tick the 4 “I can…”. Homework: plan headlight + brake-light code in Python. TA notes whose buggy followed the line.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Time to shine! — lights, synergy and the final challenge
lessons.objectives:
I can wire white headlights and red brake lights to the Pico
I can turn lights on and off at the right time in my code
I can combine motors, sensors and lights so the buggy works on its own
I can answer review questions about the whole physical computing project
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: headlight · brake light · anode (long leg) · cathode (short leg) · synergy · autonomous · sequence. TA: each pair has their buggy, two white LEDs, two red LEDs, jumper cables, Thonny open; the end-of-unit quiz ready. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud. Final lesson — pupils are more independent; the assessment rubric is used while they work.
1. (0–5) Starter — light it up! Recall last lesson’s homework: which pins, what setup, when to switch lights on/off. Pupils CHOOSE which LED leg is the anode and SORT “turn this light ON” situations on the starter worksheet.
   • Support: tick which leg is positive.  • Core: when should the brake lights come on?  • Challenge: explain ‘synergy’ for the buggy.
2. (5–20) I-do → we-do — add the lights. Anode (long leg) → a spare GPIO pin; cathode (short leg) → GND. Show the LED images. Pupils write simple on/off test code for white headlights and red brake lights, and put the ‘flash the headlights three times’ program in order (Parsons) on the activity worksheet.
   • Likely error: LED in the wrong way round → fix-words “long leg to the pin, short leg to ground.”
3. (20–50) You-do — the final challenge (Pico Grand Prix). Combine everything: flash headlights ×3, brake lights on at a standstill, wait 5 s, brake lights off and drive, keep checking the ultrasonic distance, stop + brake lights if something is in the way. Pupils ORDER the whole sequence, then build and test. Paste a screenshot of the finished buggy.
   • Support: match each light to when it is on.  • Core: complete the start sequence.  • Challenge: add line-following into the run. TA: “prompt, get ONE behaviour working before combining.”
4. (45–55) End-of-unit quiz (set separately) — exam-style review of the whole unit. Then plenary: what would you add or improve next? Self-tick the 4 “I can…”. TA notes finished buggies for the rubric.
Differentiation: the activity is on the SAME task at three depths; the quiz is a shared review everyone sits. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.
